entities.description: Electronic encoding of Bülbring's printed pp. 303-308. It preserves the article title, 190-position poem, italic typography, twenty footnotes, page and manuscript-folio milestones, explicit, separate post-explicit saying, and eleven German commentary paragraphs, but excludes Bülbring's introduction and Turnbull correction list on pp. 301-302.

bibliographic.citation: Karl Daniel Bülbring, 'Das Trentalle Sancti Gregorii in der Edinburgher Handschrift,' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 13, Neue Folge 1 (1891): 301-308.
bibliographic.title: Das Trentalle Sancti Gregorii in der Edinburgher Handschrift

entities.description: Bülbring's eight-page Anglia article: pp. 301-302 introduce his 1890 direct inspection of the Edinburgh codex and enumerate Turnbull's errors; pp. 303-308 print the complete Adv.MS.19.3.1 poem and its post-explicit saying with footnotes and German Anmerkungen. Bülbring also compares Kaufmann, a Cambridge witness he calls C, Lambeth, and Vernon readings.
entities.label: Karl Daniel Bülbring, Das Trentalle Sancti Gregorii in der Edinburgher Handschrift (1891)

rights.basis: The article was published in 1891, and the Internet Archive item metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States; current U.S. duration guidance places such publication outside copyright.

dateStatements.label: Target addition
dateStatements.value: approximately contemporary with the main codex

entities.description: A c. 1480 paper codex of nine originally independent fascicles, principally copied by Richard Heeg, with the Trental on ff. 213r-216r among approximately contemporary additions by another, unidentified hand. The poem ends with a Latin explicit and a two-line fidelity saying; the adjacent f. 216v contains the musical hymn Deus creator omnium.

holding.notes: Former Advocates Library manuscript, presented to the nation on the National Library of Scotland's foundation in 1925.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.3.1

notes: Full red morocco binding, 1964.
The target is an addition by a hand other than principal scribe Richard Heeg.

bibliographic.citation: Albert Kaufmann, Trentalle Sancti Gregorii: Eine mittelenglische Legende in zwei Texten, Erlanger Beiträge 3 (Erlangen and Leipzig: A. Deichert'sche Verlagsbuchhandlung Nachf. [Georg Böhme], 1889).
bibliographic.title: Trentalle Sancti Gregorii: Eine mittelenglische Legende in zwei Texten

entities.description: Kaufmann's 57-page Erlanger Beiträge publication distinguishes A and B versions. For B, he describes Edinburgh E as a 190-line copy with wholly missing lines 100 and 150 and Cambridge C as a 202-line copy, hypothesizes independent descent from a lost antecedent, and prints C because his E evidence came through Turnbull and J. Clark's collation rather than a reliable direct transcription.
entities.label: Albert Kaufmann, Trentalle Sancti Gregorii (1889 expanded publication)

next_steps: Consult Martin Connolly's 2011 Brogyntyn edition and subsequent Trental scholarship.
Obtain and collate complete facsimiles of CUL Kk.1.6, BL Harley 3810 Part I, NLW Brogyntyn II.6, and NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1.
Treat Kaufmann's lost B antecedent and possible oral transmission as hypotheses until tested against all witnesses.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the B-version composed, what source or sources underlie it, and what exact stemma connects its four surviving witnesses?

next_steps: Inspect the physical manuscript or preservation masters at higher resolution.
Compare the letterforms and ink with the parallel sayings on ff. 7 and 157v.
Retain Bülbring's same-hand and DIMEV's different-hand statements as attributed conflicting claims until resolved.
openQuestions.question: Was the f. 216r two-line fidelity saying written by the Trental scribe or by a different hand?

next_steps: Recheck or report the NLW handle's HTTP 500 and cache the complete Brogyntyn codex if service returns.
Search current Cambridge and British Library delivery systems before ordering new whole-object photography.
Request explicit repository, publication, and print-on-demand reuse terms for each supplied image set.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable color facsimiles of the three collateral B-version manuscripts be obtained and cached with exact work mappings?

next_steps: Ask NLS for reel-level coverage statements and whether duplication is available.
Specify covers, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, every leaf, edges if offered, and separate target mapping in any whole-object request.
Keep the complete provider-published IIIF bundle distinct from a complete physical-surface reproduction.
openQuestions.question: Do NLS microfilms 173 and 549 cover the complete codex, exactly what is missing from microfilm 699, and can preservation masters include every modern blank flyleaf omitted from public IIIF?

next_steps: Retry the stable U-M URL in an ordinary browser after the Cloudflare challenge clears.
Capture the exact item or collection rights statement without applying it backward to the NLS images or Bülbring scan.
Continue grounding content claims in the checked-in XML and source edition.

reviewNotes: The primary path is repository XML to U-M encoding to Bülbring's 1891 direct transcription to NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1, ff. 213r-216r.
Bülbring used the physical manuscript directly; Turnbull is a corrected prior edition and Kaufmann is a mediated comparison source, not a replacement source witness.
The CUL, BL, NLW, and NLS copies are four collateral witnesses of DIMEV record 4979 / IMEV 3184. Their distinct extents and readings are not collapsed into the 190-position Edinburgh version.

DIMEV's 101-couplet record description belongs to the broader witness family. The XML's 192 L elements decompose into 190 Trental positions, including two missing-line rows, plus a separately indexed two-line saying.
Canvas mapping was visually corrected: canvases 441-447 are ff. 213r-216r and contain the work; canvas 448 is f. 216v music outside the work.
Richard Heeg is the codex's principal scribe but not the target Trental scribe according to the current NLS catalog.
NLS item images carry an exact Public Domain Mark 1.0 statement; BSB Kaufmann images carry NoC-NC 1.0; DIMEV and Gordon are CC BY 4.0. These statements remain attached to their exact access layers.

All publicly obtainable supporting files used intensively were cached whole where possible and hard-linked into the main checkout's ignored source cache. The IA Kaufmann PDF is correctly recorded as partial despite its pre-existing filename, and the NLW complete digital handle could not be cached because it returned HTTP 500.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00102 XML is a repository copy of a University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English encoding derived from Karl Daniel Bülbring's 1891 Anglia article on the anonymous B-version of the Trentalle Sancti Gregorii in National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.3.1, ff. 213r-216r. Bülbring directly inspected and transcribed the manuscript, corrected William Turnbull's 1843 printing, and consulted Albert Kaufmann's 1889 version study. The XML starts at Bülbring's printed p. 303, omitting the article's pp. 301-302 introduction and Turnbull correction list, and retains the 190-position poem, twenty footnotes, a separately indexed two-line post-explicit scribal saying, and German commentary through p. 308. A complete 482-canvas NLS IIIF bundle maps the target to canvases 441-447; canvas 448 is adjacent f. 216v music and is outside the work. DIMEV v1.0 distinguishes the four IMEV 3184 B-version witnesses and the two-line saying, while Kaufmann and current scholarship distinguish other Trental versions. Complete public source objects are cached where obtainable, including the NLS IIIF object, Anglia volume, DIMEV release, BSB Kaufmann dissertation object, and Gordon's 2026 open-access article; the cached Internet Archive Kaufmann book scan is explicitly partial because printed pp. 18-25 are absent.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: By the first half of the fifteenth century; exact composition date unresolved; represented witness c. 1480
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of composition and dialect region unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Trentalle Sancti Gregorii

catalogingSubject.label: Trentalle Sancti Gregorii
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English B-version of the Trental of Saint Gregory as represented by the approximately 1480 copy in National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.3.1, ff. 213r-216r. Bülbring numbers this witness through line 190, including two displayed positions for wholly missing lines; the separately indexed two-line scribal saying after the explicit and Bülbring's German commentary are represented as scoped source components, not absorbed into the medieval poem's authorship, language, form, or extent. Other B-version witnesses, other Trental recensions, the physical codex, editions, encodings, and facsimiles remain separate layers.

contentParts.label: Trentalle Sancti Gregorii

locators.value: DIV1 TYPE='poem', LG, first 190 L elements

notes: The two dotted rows at line positions 100 and 150 are retained in the 190-position count; Bülbring explicitly notes the missing rhyme lines.
contentParts.summary: The represented Adv.MS.19.3.1 B-version: Gregory's mother confesses, returns from purgatorial suffering, prescribes a trental, and after its performance returns in bliss.

contentParts.label: Be it trowe or be it fals

locators.value: p. 308, after line 190 and the explicit

notes: Bülbring's footnote says the accompanying writing is in the same hand; DIMEV v1.0 says the saying is in a different hand from the Trental. Direct inspection of the 1600-pixel image did not settle the conflict.
contentParts.summary: A two-line saying asserting fidelity to the copy, written after the Trental explicit and separately indexed by DIMEV as record 762 / IMEV 467.5.

contentParts.label: Anmerkungen
notes: The single commentary section contains eleven encoded paragraphs. Editorial German is not a language or prose-form property of the medieval poem.
contentParts.summary: Bülbring's German linguistic and textual notes on selected lines, including comparisons with Turnbull, Kaufmann, Cambridge witness C, Lambeth, and Vernon readings.

assertion.notes: The terminus comes from a B-version witness, not from equating the c. 1480 Edinburgh copy with composition. Gordon calls the Brogyntyn B-version witness c. 1400, while the current NLW catalog dates the codex to the first half of the fifteenth century.

dateStatements.display: By the first half of the fifteenth century; exact composition date unresolved
First half of the fifteenth century (NLW Brogyntyn MS II.6)

extent.description: The XML has two DIV1 sections: the poem division contains one LG with 192 L elements, of which 190 belong to Bülbring's numbered Trental lineation and two belong to a separate scribal saying; the second division contains eleven German commentary P elements. The poem's 190 positions include two dotted rows marking wholly missing lines, so 188 positions contain verbal text. Also encoded are two HEAD, twenty NOTE, six PB, fifty-five MILESTONE, two CLOSER, one TRAILER, one EPIGRAPH, one Q, and 347 HI elements.

formStatements.description: Rhyming couplets (aa). Bülbring's represented version has 190 numbered line positions, including two rows that display wholly missing lines; 188 positions contain verbal text. The following two-line scribal saying and prose commentary are separately scoped source components.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Brief embedded and paratextual Latin does not make the poem mixed-form.

scope.description: Latin title and explicit, liturgical terms such as Placebo and dirige, and Amen

notes: The poem is anonymous. Saint Gregory is a narrative figure and traditional title referent, not a documented author.
Richard Heeg or Heege is the principal scribe of the larger codex, but the NLS catalog places the Trental among approximately contemporary additions by another, unidentified hand.

The XML begins with Bülbring's article title on printed p. 303 and omits the article introduction and detailed Turnbull correction list on pp. 301-302; it retains the poem, post-explicit saying, footnotes, and Anmerkungen through p. 308.
The XML's 192 L elements do not mean a 192-line Trental: 190 are the witness-version lineation, including two dotted missing-line positions, and two are the separately indexed scribal saying.
DIMEV's description of 101 couplets is a record-level statement for the broader IMEV 3184 tradition and must not overwrite the 95-couplet-position / 190-line Edinburgh version.
Bülbring's German commentary and the publication's German title matter are editorial layers; the medieval poem remains Middle English verse with brief embedded Latin.

Complete source caches include the provider's entire 482-canvas NLS image sequence, the complete Anglia volume and OCR, DIMEV v1.0, the complete BSB Kaufmann dissertation object, and Gordon's complete article HTML. The Internet Archive Kaufmann PDF is explicitly partial because its scan omits printed pp. 18-25.

next_steps: Consult Martin Connolly's 2011 Brogyntyn edition and later work-specific scholarship.
Collate all four B-version witnesses directly, keeping witness date and dialect distinct from composition.
Do not promote Kaufmann's lost B antecedent or oral-transmission suggestion from historical hypothesis to confirmed exemplar.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the B-version composed, what source or sources underlie it, and how exactly are its four DIMEV witnesses related?

next_steps: Inspect the physical manuscript or a higher-resolution preservation image under raking or multispectral light if available.
Compare the f. 216r hand with the parallel sayings on ff. 7 and 157v.
Preserve Bülbring's same-hand report and DIMEV's different-hand report as attributed, conflicting claims.
openQuestions.question: Was the f. 216r 'Be it trowe' saying written by the Trental scribe or by a different hand?

next_steps: Resolve the NLW handle's current HTTP failure and, if restored, cache the complete codex rather than ff. 94v-96 alone.
Check or request whole-object facsimiles from Cambridge and the British Library before ordering excerpts.
Obtain shelfmark-specific reuse statements before redistribution.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, openly reusable color facsimiles of CUL Kk.1.6, BL Harley 3810 Part I, and NLW Brogyntyn II.6 be obtained and mapped precisely?

assertion.notes: The NLS catalog's North Midlands description concerns the codex and principal scribe, while the target poem is an approximately contemporary addition by another hand; the Pricke dialect localization in the Brogyntyn catalog likewise must not be transferred to this Trental without work-specific evidence.

scope.description: Broad cultural and linguistic context of the Middle English poem

assertion.notes: The title invokes Gregory and the narrative makes him a character; neither fact establishes Gregory as author.

notes: Edition-level responsibility, not medieval authorship.

subjects.label: Purgatory, masses, and prayer for the dead
Secret sin, confession, penance, and salvation
The restless dead and ghostly return

summaries.value: Pope Gregory's mother, revered for outward holiness, confesses that shame kept her from revealing secret sexual sin and the killing of three children. After death she returns as a tormented, foul-smelling ghost and asks Gregory to perform a prescribed trental of thirty masses, the Office of the Dead, and prayer. When the observance is completed she returns radiant among angels and announces her release into bliss. CME00102 represents Bülbring's 190-line Edinburgh-witness version and his commentary; a following two-line statement about fidelity to the copy is a separately indexed scribal saying, not part of the poem.

notes: Normalized discovery form; the represented witness reads 'Some tyme in Rome a pope þer was'.
titles.value: Some time in Rome a pope there was

scope.description: Explicit of the represented Adv.MS.19.3.1 version

work.summary: Pope Gregory's mother, revered for outward holiness, confesses that shame kept her from revealing secret sexual sin and the killing of three children. After death she returns as a tormented, foul-smelling ghost and asks Gregory to perform a prescribed trental of thirty masses, the Office of the Dead, and prayer. When the observance is completed she returns radiant among angels and announces her release into bliss. CME00102 represents Bülbring's 190-line Edinburgh-witness version and his commentary; a following two-line statement about fidelity to the copy is a separately indexed scribal saying, not part of the poem.
